title: 荣誉附件正文｜LOA full（第5部分）

This is particularly important if we want to deploy sentiment analysis training close to the users (e. g. , on their mobile phone). Considering the huge amount of data storage and transmission overhead, we simply cannot collect all the data sources before training. As a concrete example, Cyber threats are hard to identify and predict because the hackers that conduct these attacks often obfuscate their activity and intentions. However, they may still use publicly accessible forums discuss vulnerabilities and share tradecraft about how to exploit them. The behavior of the hacker community, as expressed in such venues, may provide insights into group’s malicious intent [13]. To this end, the proposed project can be applied to predict malicious cyber events by exploiting malicious actor’s behavior via sentiment analysis of posts on hacker forums in real-time. A-3 RTDS S 21 14 01 1 Work Package 1 Work Package 2 Work Package 3 Comprehensive Online Sentiment Learning High-Performance System Benchmarking Study: Algorithm: • A benchmark with • Filtering and adaptive Implementation: representative application batching to amortize system • Periodical model merging to domains call overhead improve system concurrency • Implementing competitive • Contextual-aware fast word with minimum affecting of solutions including both embedding accuracy lexicon-based and learning- • Word centroid distance • LRU-based model pruning to based solutions similarity-based classification reduce model access and • Detailed profiling study • Spatial-temporal-aware trend management overhead considering accuracy, detection feedback throughput, and latency Research Proposal: Online Sentiment Learning of Massive Data Streams Figure 1 Research Overview e. Technical Description of the Seed Project: • Methodology & Approach The major thrusts in this proposal are to: 1) propose an efficient online sentiment learning algorithm, 2) implement the proposed algorithm based on a modern stream processing system, 3) design a comprehensive benchmark suite and conduct a detailed profiling study to evaluate the proposed solution against the state-of-the-art. As shown in Figure 1, they are grouped into three work packages accordingly. We will describe the details of those thrusts one by one. Figure 2 Algorithm Overview First, An Online Sentiment Learning Algorithm: The high-level overview of our algorithm is shown in Figure 2. It consists of two main modules. On the left, we have the pre-processing module, and the online learning module is shown on the right. A) Filtering and adaptive batching. The pre-processing module has three components: 1) to reduce workload, the newly arrived tweets are first pre-processed and the pre- processor filters out some vocabularies that have nothing to do with emotions; 2) to further reduce the influence caused by tense, singular, and plural, and deformation, etc, we perform stemming on the cleaned input stream; 3) training each tweet one by one involves frequent access to the model and leads to significant data access overhead. Thus, we propose to batch a small number (tuneable) of tweets periodically before training to amortize the data access overhead.
